title: Pharmacology Fundamentals — USMLE Step 1
description: Pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, receptor pharmacology, autonomic drugs, anesthetics, and toxicology essentials.
subject: medicine

cards.back: Vd = amount of drug in body / plasma concentration. High Vd = drug distributed into tissues (lipophilic). Low Vd = stays in plasma (hydrophilic, protein-bound)
Zero-order: constant amount eliminated per unit time (rate independent of concentration). Examples: ethanol, phenytoin, aspirin (high dose). First-order: constant fraction eliminated per unit time
t½ = 0.693 × Vd / CL. Steady state reached in ~4–5 half-lives
LD = (Cp target × Vd) / bioavailability. Used to rapidly achieve therapeutic level when immediate effect needed (e.g., digoxin, aminoglycosides)
Fraction of administered drug that reaches systemic circulation unchanged. IV = 100%. Oral varies. F = AUC oral / AUC IV

cards.front: Phase I vs Phase II drug metabolism
cards.back: Phase I: CYP450 oxidation/reduction/hydrolysis → adds functional group. Phase II: conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation, acetylation, methylation) → adds polar group for excretion. Phase I products can be active
SPCCC RF: St. John's Wort, Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Chronic alcohol, Cigarettes, Rifampin, Griseofulvin, Barbiturates
SICKFACES.COM: Sulfonamides, Isoniazid, Cimetidine, Ketoconazole, Fluoxetine, Acute alcohol, Chloramphenicol, Erythromycin/ciprofloxacin, Amiodarone, Metronidazole

cards.front: Therapeutic index: definition and drugs with narrow TI
cards.back: TI = LD50/ED50 (or toxic dose / effective dose). Narrow TI drugs: digoxin, lithium, phenytoin, theophylline, warfarin, aminoglycosides, vancomycin

cards.front: Agonist vs antagonist vs partial agonist
cards.back: Agonist: full efficacy (Emax). Partial agonist: submaximal efficacy even at saturation. Competitive antagonist: shifts dose-response right (same Emax). Noncompetitive: decreases Emax
Muscarinic (M1-M3) antagonist. Uses: bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning, mydriasis, antispasmodic. Side effects: dry mouth, urinary retention, tachycardia, blurred vision, ↑ IOP, constipation
Direct muscarinic agonist. Used for glaucoma (decreases IOP) and xerostomia (Sjögren's syndrome)
Physostigmine: tertiary amine → crosses BBB → used for anticholinergic toxicity (central). Neostigmine: quaternary → does not cross BBB → used for myasthenia gravis, reversal of NMJ blockade
Non-selective (β1 + β2 blocker). β2 blockade → bronchoconstriction (avoid in asthma/COPD) + inhibits glycogenolysis (masks hypoglycemia signs in diabetics)
Metoprolol, Atenolol, Esmolol, Acebutolol, Betaxolol. Mnemonic: 'A BEAM' — Acebutolol, Betaxolol, Esmolol, Atenolol, Metoprolol
α1: vasoconstriction (↑ BP), ↑ sphincter tone, mydriasis. α2: presynaptic autoreceptor → inhibits NE release (central α2 agonists: clonidine, methyldopa → ↓ BP)
Pure α1 agonist. Use: decongestant, hypotension (reflex bradycardia). No β effects
Low dose: β effects (↑HR, ↑contractility, vasodilation β2). High dose: α1 effects dominate (vasoconstriction, ↑BP). Used for anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest, mixed with local anesthetics
Nicotinic (Nm, Nn): ionotropic, Na+/K+ channel — at NMJ and autonomic ganglia. Muscarinic (M1–M5): metabotropic GPCR — at effector organs
Block nicotinic receptors in all autonomic ganglia (sympathetic and parasympathetic). Example: hexamethonium. Historically used for hypertension; now mainly research

cards.front: Non-depolarizing NMJ blockers: mechanism and reversal
cards.back: Competitive antagonists at nicotinic NMJ (tubocurarine, vecuronium, rocuronium, atracurium). Reversal: neostigmine + glycopyrrolate (or sugammadex for rocuronium)
Depolarizing NMJ blocker (mimics ACh, not reversed by AChE inhibitors). Fastest onset/offset. Complications: malignant hyperthermia (↑Ca2+ from SR), hyperkalemia in burns/muscle disease

cards.front: Local anesthetics: mechanism and order of block
cards.back: Block voltage-gated Na+ channels (inner aspect). Order: small myelinated (pain/temp) → small unmyelinated → large myelinated (motor). Amides (lidocaine, bupivacaine): metabolized by liver. Esters (cocaine, procaine): metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase
Class IB antiarrhythmic — blocks Na+ channels preferentially in ischemic tissue. Used for ventricular arrhythmias post-MI. Toxicity: CNS (seizures, tinnitus) before cardiovascular
Tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, red wine, cured meats), sympathomimetics, meperidine (avoid — risk of serotonin syndrome/hypertensive crisis)

cards.front: Serotonin syndrome vs NMS: distinguishing features
cards.back: Serotonin syndrome: neuromuscular excitability (tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus), agitation, diarrhea, onset HOURS. NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, ↑CK, onset DAYS after antipsychotic change

cards.front: Acetaminophen overdose: mechanism of toxicity and antidote
cards.back: NAPQI (toxic metabolite) depletes glutathione → centrilobular hepatic necrosis. Antidote: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — replenishes glutathione
Early: primary respiratory alkalosis (stimulates respiratory center → hyperventilation). Late: primary metabolic acidosis (↑ anion gap from organic acid accumulation)

Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI cramps, Emesis (muscarinic). + Nicotinic: muscle weakness/fasciculations. Treatment: atropine (muscarinic) + pralidoxime (2-PAM, reactivates AChE if given early)
Miosis (pinpoint pupils), respiratory depression, CNS depression. Reversal: naloxone (μ-opioid receptor antagonist)

cards.front: Benzodiazepine vs barbiturate: GABA-A receptor mechanism
cards.back: Benzodiazepines: ↑ frequency of Cl− channel opening. Barbiturates: ↑ duration of Cl− channel opening. Both enhance GABA effect (barbiturates at higher dose can open channel directly)
Flumazenil (GABA-A receptor antagonist). Short duration → resedation possible. Do NOT use if patient is benzodiazepine-dependent (precipitates seizures)
Stop digoxin. Correct K+, Mg2+. Bradycardia/heart block: atropine or temporary pacing. VT/VF: anti-digoxin Fab fragments (Digibind). Avoid calcium (↑ cardiotoxicity)

cards.front: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs): clinical uses and adverse effects
cards.back: Block AT1 receptor. Uses: hypertension, HF, diabetic nephropathy (like ACE inhibitors). No cough (bradykinin unaffected) — but can still cause angioedema. Avoid in pregnancy

cards.front: Nitric oxide: mechanism and clinical use
cards.back: Produced from L-arginine by NOS → activates guanylyl cyclase → ↑ cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation (vasodilation). Inhaled NO: pulmonary vasodilation in ARDS/pulmonary HTN
PDE-5 inhibitor → prevents cGMP breakdown → vasodilation (penile and pulmonary). DANGEROUS: combined with nitrates → severe hypotension (both ↑ cGMP)
Cross BBB → sedation. Anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention). Examples: diphenhydramine, chlorpheniramine, promethazine
Irreversibly inhibit H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) on parietal cells. Most effective acid suppression. Long-term: ↓ B12 absorption, ↑ risk of C. diff, hypomagnesemia
Mucosal protection (inhibits acid, stimulates mucus/bicarbonate), cervical ripening, medical abortion (with mifepristone). Contraindicated in pregnancy (causes uterine contractions)
5-HT3 antagonist. Anti-emetic for chemotherapy-induced, post-operative, and radiation nausea. Side effect: prolonged QT interval
HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors → ↓ cholesterol synthesis, ↑ LDL receptors. Side effects: myopathy (↑ CK) → rarely rhabdomyolysis. ↑ risk with fibrates, cyclosporine, CYP3A4 inhibitors
PPARα activation → ↑ lipoprotein lipase → ↓ triglycerides (most potent TG lowering), modest ↑ HDL. Risk: myopathy (↑ with statins), gallstones (↑ cholesterol in bile)

cards.front: Niacin (nicotinic acid): uses and side effects
cards.back: ↑ HDL, ↓ triglycerides, ↓ LDL. Side effect: flushing (prostaglandin-mediated, reduced by aspirin). Contraindicated in gout, liver disease, diabetes (↑ glucose)

cards.front: Thiazide diuretics: metabolic side effects (6 Hs)
cards.back: Hypokalemia, Hyponatremia, Hyperlipidemia, Hyperglycemia, Hyperuricemia, Hypercalcemia
Aldosterone receptor antagonist (K+-sparing diuretic). Side effects: hyperkalemia, gynecomastia, anti-androgenic effects. Eplerenone is more selective (less gynecomastia)

cards.front: NSAIDs: mechanism of action and GI protection
cards.back: Non-selective COX-1/COX-2 inhibition → ↓ prostaglandins → ↓ pain/inflammation; GI mucosal damage (↓ PGE2). Co-prescribe PPI or misoprostol for GI protection

cards.front: Antiepileptic drugs: mechanism and clinical uses by drug class
cards.back: Na+ channel blockers: phenytoin (status epilepticus; zero-order kinetics), carbamazepine (partial seizures, trigeminal neuralgia), valproate, lamotrigine. GABA enhancers: benzodiazepines, phenobarbital. Ca2+ channel (T-type): ethosuximide (absence seizures — drug of choice). Valproate: broad spectrum, teratogenic (neural tube defects). Levetiracetam: binds SV2A, minimal drug interactions.

cards.front: Beta-blockers: selective vs non-selective and clinical applications
cards.back: Selective β1 (MIAMI): Metoprolol, Atenolol, Bisoprolol, Esmolol (shortest acting — IV use), Acebutolol. Non-selective: propranolol (also ↓ renin), carvedilol (α1+β), labetalol (α1+β, IV in hypertensive emergency in pregnancy). Indications: HTN, angina, HF (carvedilol/metoprolol/bisoprolol), post-MI. Contraindications: asthma (bronchospasm), Prinzmetal angina. Abrupt withdrawal: rebound tachycardia.

cards.front: ACE inhibitors vs ARBs: mechanisms, differences, and shared contraindications
cards.back: ACEi: block ACE → ↓angiotensin II + ↓bradykinin degradation. ARB: block AT1 receptor (no bradykinin effect). ACEi side effects: dry cough (bradykinin), angioedema (rare, risk with ARB overlap). ARBs: no cough. Shared: hyperkalemia, ↑creatinine (↓efferent tone → ↓GFR), contraindicated in bilateral RAS, pregnancy (fetal renal toxicity). Benefit: HF, DM nephropathy, post-MI, CKD proteinuria.

UFH: activates antithrombin III → inhibits IIa + Xa (monitor aPTT; reverse with protamine). LMWH: anti-Xa > anti-IIa (monitor anti-Xa; partial reversal with protamine). Warfarin: inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase → ↓II, VII, IX, X, protein C/S (monitor INR; reverse with vitamin K, FFP, or 4-factor PCC). DOACs: apixaban/rivaroxaban (anti-Xa), dabigatran (direct thrombin; reversal: idarucizumab); factor Xa reversal: andexanet alfa.
